Self-determination theory (SDT) is a theory of motivation ( Deci & Ryan, 1985, 2000 ), a theory about external and internal factors and their interrelationships that drive people to act. External factors could be reward or punishment, internal factors fun or curiosity. A detailed review of the theory can be found in  Ünlü (2023) , this reference is subsequently referred to as MSDT1, which is the first of the two papers on the mathematical underpinnings of the theory. This follow-up second paper is referred to as MSDT2.

An informal representation of SDT can be found in  Table 1  (for details on the concepts contained therein, see MSDT1).

Briefly, intrinsic motivation or intrinsic regulation is behavior characterized by volitional internal factors, such as genuine interest. Extrinsic motivation can be the regulation types of external regulation, introjected regulation, and identified regulation. External regulation describes behavior that is dictated by fully external factors, such as money. Introjected regulation includes behavior enacted by external societal expectations, such as shame. Identified regulation occurs if a person identifies with the value of an activity, but not enjoys it. Finally, amotivation or non-regulation is the lack of any form of regulated (intrinsic and extrinsic) motivation.

These regulations have their different internalities (locus of causality), which are fully external and fully internal for external regulation and intrinsic regulation, respectively, external and internal for the intermediate regulations, and impersonal for non-regulation. With more internalized regulations, the corresponding behaviors are more self-determined.

In MSDT1, in the real representation, we have redefined and unified these motivations based on their decomposability into internal motivation, external motivation, and amotivation. Thereby, we have embedded the common motivations into a broader continuous spectrum of graded motivations. In this paper, similar results will be derived, but in the more general affine space representation of the theory.

We recapitulate the main results obtained in MSDT1 later in a separate section of this paper, which will discuss the connections between this work and MSDT1. What has been discussed in greater length in MSDT1 were also the limitations of our approach and the motivations for it. This will not be repeated in this paper.

The real representation of the theory in the lower half of the unit square (Fig. 5 in MSDT1) can be generalized. We present a generalization in real affine spaces with underlying vector spaces over the field of real numbers (or reals)  
                         R 
                      . More abstract fields with successively stronger restricting properties such as ordered, Archimedean ordered, or complete Archimedean ordered fields could be considered. Not all results of the simpler representation can then be retained. For example, in Section 4.2 in MSDT1, to derive the lattice-theoretic properties, we have relied on the assumption that the supremum and infimum of any non-empty and, respectively, bounded from above and below subset of  
                         R 
                       exist. This may not be satisfied anymore in more general fields.

This paper is rather of theoretical interest. The representation in MSDT1, which is a special case, is a direct and complete conceptualization of SDT, a simplest choice of representation (cf. Section  5 ). The following concepts about affine spaces are standard in geometry (e.g.,  Gallier, 2001; Tarrida, 2011 ).

This paper is structured as follows. In Section  2 , we review the necessary basic concepts of affine geometry. In Sections  3 and 4 , we generalize the model in reals to represent SDT in more abstract affine spaces. This includes, in Section  3 , the primitives for the representation and the motivation  
                         2 
                      -simplex, regulated and graded motivation, and self-determination, and in Section  4 , polar coordinates in (Euclidean) affine motivation spaces, and the study of self-determination on radial and angular line segments. In Section  5 , we prove the distributivity of the lattice of general self-determination, then discuss the special case real representation of MSDT1, and show that different choices of primitives for a description of the theory imply equivalent representations. In particular, the representation in reals is seen to be a simplest canonical choice, in the sense that all resulting motivation simplexes are naturally isomorphic to it. In Section  6 , we summarize this paper and conclude with a resume.

Remark 1 
                         A remark regarding notation for the remainder of this paper is in order. The symbol  
                               ϕ 
                             stands for the group homomorphism and should not be confused with the polar coordinate used in the real representation in MSDT1. To highlight correspondence, we will also denote with same symbols (e.g.,  
                               A 
                             and  
                               M 
                            ) the affine space analogs of the real representation concepts. If not stated otherwise, with those symbols, we will always refer to affine space definitions.

                            , which is independent of  
                               
                                  a 
                                  ∈ 
                                  M 
                               
                            ) is also called the convex combination of the weighted points  
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           a

                                     ,

                                        a

                                  , 
                                  …

                                        a

                             are affinely independent (below), the scalars

                                  … 
                                  ,

                             are called the (barycentric) coordinates of the point  
                               
                                  x 
                                  =

                                        =

Remark 2 
                         Note that the assumption  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ∑ 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                        = 
                                        1 
                                     
                                     
                                        n 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        λ 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  1 
                               
                             on the scalars suffices for an affine combination to be well-defined. The weights do not need to be non-negative. For our purposes, however, only convex combinations are considered. For three affinely independent points distinguished as primitives of the formalization, representing the extreme points of unit internal, external, and impersonal loci, we define the regulations of motivation to be the convex combinations of those reference points, that is, the  
                               2 
                            -simplex spanned by them. In particular, there is ambiguity in the choice of the primitive points. However, all possible choices of primitive points imply equivalent representations of the theory, since canonically the resulting simplexes are isomorphic (Section  5.3 ).

Note that the assumption  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ∑ 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                        = 
                                        1 
                                     
                                     
                                        n 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        λ 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  1 
                               
                             on the scalars suffices for an affine combination to be well-defined. The weights do not need to be non-negative. For our purposes, however, only convex combinations are considered. For three affinely independent points distinguished as primitives of the formalization, representing the extreme points of unit internal, external, and impersonal loci, we define the regulations of motivation to be the convex combinations of those reference points, that is, the  
                               2 
                            -simplex spanned by them. In particular, there is ambiguity in the choice of the primitive points. However, all possible choices of primitive points imply equivalent representations of the theory, since canonically the resulting simplexes are isomorphic (Section  5.3 ).

We need the notion of affine independence.

Definition 4

                                  + 
                                  1

                               M 
                            . These points are affinely independent iff the (different) vectors

                                              0

                                  ¯ 
                               
                            .

Convex combinations of affinely independent points have unique weights.

Lemma 5

                                  1

                                              0

Identifying the regulations of motivation in simplexes of an affine space, we may be interested in the notion of a distance between the points of an affine space, as the measure of closeness of regulations. The canonical metric induced by a norm on the associated (real) vector space of an affine space can be used.

This induced metric is invariant under the action of the normed affine space. Stated differently, if

                               ( 
                               a 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                      , the induced metric is translation-invariant.

How to represent SDT in concepts of an affine space? The goal of this section is to show that the discussion of MSDT1 in  
                         
                            { 
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               , 
                               y 
                               ) 
                            
                            ∈ 
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            : 
                            x 
                            , 
                            y 
                            ≥ 
                            0 
                            , 
                            x 
                            + 
                            y 
                            ≤ 
                            1 
                            } 
                         
                       can be recapped analogously in more general affine spaces. However (Section  5.3 ), the different representations are equivalent, that is, isomorphic simplex structures. This gives justification for the representation in reals, as put forth in MSDT1, which is unique up to isomorphic copies and a simplest conceptualization among these. This also seems to corroborate the significance of SDT as a, uniquely, mathematically describable theory.

Subsequently, let the affine space  
                            
                               ( 
                               M 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     M 
                                  
                                  ¯ 
                               
                               , 
                               ϕ 
                               ) 
                            
                          be given. We posit the primitives for the representation.

Primitives.  Distinguish three affinely independent points  
                            
                               i 
                               , 
                               e 
                               , 
                               p 
                               ∈ 
                               M 
                            
                         , referred to as the internal locus, external locus, and impersonal locus, also denoted by  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     l 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     l 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     l 
                                  
                                  
                                     3 
                                  
                               
                            
                         , respectively. These loci are called the base points of the representation.

Based on the primitives, we define the notions of the representation.

Lemma 8 
                            
                               The motivation space is a convex subset of the set of points, that is, every convex combination of any two regulations is a regulation. Stated differently, the motivation space is closed under convex combination.

The motivation space is a convex subset of the set of points, that is, every convex combination of any two regulations is a regulation. Stated differently, the motivation space is closed under convex combination.

Remark 3 
                            Obviously,

                                  i

                                  e 
                               , and  
                                  p 
                               , then this subset must also contain all convex combinations of these points. That is,  
                                  A

The following notion is central, that is why we summarize it in a definition.

Definition 7 
                            
                               
                                  A 
                                is the  
                                  2 
                               -simplex spanned or determined by the three vertexes  
                                  i 
                               ,  
                                  e 
                               , and  
                                  p 
                               .

A 
                                is the  
                                  2 
                               -simplex spanned or determined by the three vertexes  
                                  i 
                               ,  
                                  e 
                               , and  
                                  p 
                               .

We may interpret this simplex geometrically. Since the points  
                            i 
                         ,  
                            e 
                         , and  
                            p 
                          are affinely independent, they are not collinear, that is, not on the same line. These points are contained in a unique plane and delineate the triangle with the vertexes  
                            i 
                         ,  
                            e 
                         , and  
                            p 
                         , in interior and boundary (the edges) points.

Given the motivation simplex  
                            A 
                         , we can locate any regulation in its unique (barycentric) coordinates with respect to the three loci. This introduces important interpretation of loci and corresponding coordinates.

Results that we have discussed in the real representation in MSDT1 can be translated into the affine space formulation. This is what we do next.

The regulated case, without amotivation, deserves special treatment, as it is the important case in practice.

In the regulated space, the two loci  
                            i 
                          and  
                            e 
                          are complementary, in shares. The regulated motivation space is partitioned into intrinsic motivation and extrinsic motivation, and extrinsic motivation is partitioned into the external regulation type, introjected regulation type, balanced regulation type, and identified regulation type.

How to define (a measure of) self-determination in the regulated case in affine space? There are at least two possibilities. One is an application of the definition of the measure  
                            
                               S 
                               D 
                            
                          in real representation (Definition 6 in MSDT1) to barycentric coordinates in the  
                            1 
                         -simplex  
                            M 
                         .

                            A 
                          may also be referred to as the graded motivation space.

Definition 16 
                            Amotivation is defined to be  
                                  
                                     A 
                                     ∖ 
                                     M 
                                  
                               , that is, the complement of the regulated motivation space, and the regulation types of amotivation are the non-regulation types of degrees

                                     < 
                                     u 
                                     ≤ 
                                     1 
                                  
                               .

Amotivation is defined to be  
                                  
                                     A 
                                     ∖ 
                                     M 
                                  
                               , that is, the complement of the regulated motivation space, and the regulation types of amotivation are the non-regulation types of degrees  
                                  
                                     0 
                                     < 
                                     u 
                                     ≤ 
                                     1 
                                  
                               .

Thus, the (entire) motivation space is partitioned into intrinsic motivation, extrinsic motivation, and amotivation. Amotivation is partitioned into the non-regulation types of positive degrees. Hence, the motivation space is partitioned into the regulation types of intrinsic regulation, external regulation, introjected regulation, balanced regulation, identified regulation, and positive degree non-regulations.

We use the following terminology (see also  Definition 9 ).

In future work, these self-determination partial orders could be studied statistically (Section  6.3 ).

A reparametrization in variants of polar coordinates of the barycentric triangle representation of the motivation space is presented; more generally, in Euclidean affine space, that is, normed affine space whose norm is induced by a scalar product on the associated vector space. We can use this representation to describe or characterize self-determination in alternative coordinates.

By  Theorem 1 , to each pair of polar coordinates, there exists a unique triple of corresponding barycentric coordinates. How to obtain the barycentric coordinates from the polar coordinates is summarized in the next corollary, which is a consequence of the proof of  Theorem 1 .

In particular, the respective results in Section  3.3  apply. For example,

In Section  3.4 , we have seen that, on the entire motivation space, the strong, weak, and general self-determination partial orders are not complete. However, they are complete when restricted to angular, radial, and angular and radial line segments, respectively. These results are the affine space analogs of results presented in Section 4.1 in MSDT1.

Theorem 2 
                            
                               Let  
                               
                                  A 
                                
                               be the motivation space of the Euclidean affine space  
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     M 
                                     , 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              M 
                                           
                                           ¯ 
                                        
                                        , 
                                        
                                           〈 
                                           , 
                                           〉 
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     , 
                                     ϕ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               with  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        〈 
                                        
                                           
                                              i 
                                              p 
                                           
                                           ¯ 
                                        
                                        , 
                                        
                                           
                                              e 
                                              p 
                                           
                                           ¯ 
                                        
                                        〉 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     0 
                                  
                                
                               for primitives  
                               
                                  
                                     i 
                                     , 
                                     e 
                                     , 
                                     p 
                                     ∈ 
                                     A 
                                  
                               
                               . It holds: 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        1. 
                                     
                                     
                                        Strong self-determination  
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 ⪯ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 s 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        restricted to any angular line segment is a total order. 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        2. 
                                     
                                     
                                        Weak self-determination  
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 ⪯ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 w 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        restricted to any radial line segment is a total order. 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                            
                               In particular, general self-determination  
                               
                                  ⪯ 
                                
                               is complete on any angular line segment and any radial line segment.

Let  
                               
                                  A 
                                
                               be the motivation space of the Euclidean affine space  
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     M 
                                     , 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              M 
                                           
                                           ¯ 
                                        
                                        , 
                                        
                                           〈 
                                           , 
                                           〉 
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     , 
                                     ϕ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               with  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        〈 
                                        
                                           
                                              i 
                                              p 
                                           
                                           ¯ 
                                        
                                        , 
                                        
                                           
                                              e 
                                              p 
                                           
                                           ¯ 
                                        
                                        〉 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     0 
                                  
                                
                               for primitives  
                               
                                  
                                     i 
                                     , 
                                     e 
                                     , 
                                     p 
                                     ∈ 
                                     A 
                                  
                               
                               . It holds:

1. 
                                     
                                     
                                        Strong self-determination  
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 ⪯ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 s 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        restricted to any angular line segment is a total order. 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        2. 
                                     
                                     
                                        Weak self-determination  
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 ⪯ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 w 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        restricted to any radial line segment is a total order.

Strong self-determination  
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 ⪯ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 s 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        restricted to any angular line segment is a total order.

Weak self-determination  
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 ⪯ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 w 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        restricted to any radial line segment is a total order.

In particular, general self-determination  
                               
                                  ⪯ 
                                
                               is complete on any angular line segment and any radial line segment.

Proof 
                            1. Let  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           A 
                                        
                                        
                                           c 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        a

We could generalize to affine spaces virtually all of the remaining results (e.g., algebraic representation, polar characterization, dependency strength) obtained in the formulation in  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       in MSDT1. Instead, exemplarily, we only focus on the lattice structure of general self-determination, and motivated by a reviewer’s comment, present a new result on the distributivity of this lattice. To verify the other results is left to the reader. In particular, we also highlight that there exist isomorphisms between the real and (among the) different (in the choices of primitives) affine simplex structures.

The proof of the next theorem follows the line of reasoning given in Section 4.2 in MSDT1.

Theorem 3 
                            
                               Let  
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     M 
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           M 
                                        
                                        ¯ 
                                     
                                     , 
                                     ϕ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               be an affine space, not necessarily Euclidean. Let  
                               
                                  A 
                                
                               be a motivation space defined for affinely independent points  
                               
                                  
                                     i 
                                     , 
                                     e 
                                     , 
                                     p 
                                     ∈ 
                                     M 
                                  
                               
                               , with  
                               
                                  ⪯ 
                                
                               the general self-determination relation on  
                               
                                  A 
                               
                               . Then, the partially ordered set  
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     A 
                                     , 
                                     ⪯ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               is a complete lattice. In particular, this lattice is bounded, with the minimum  
                               
                                  p 
                                
                               and maximum  
                               
                                  i 
                               
                               .

                                satisfies  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           ˆ 
                                        
                                     
                                     ∈ 
                                     B 
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           ˆ 
                                        
                                     
                                     ⪯ 
                                     y 
                                  
                                for all  
                                  
                                     y 
                                     ∈ 
                                     B 
                                  
                               . Hence,  
                                  
                                     sup 
                                     X 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           ˆ 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               . In particular,  
                                  
                                     sup 
                                     A 
                                     = 
                                     inf 
                                     0̸ 
                                  
                                exists. Obviously,  
                                  
                                     i 
                                     ∈ 
                                     A 
                                  
                                is the maximum of the partially ordered set  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     A 
                                     , 
                                     ⪯ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               , that is,  
                                  
                                     a 
                                     ⪯ 
                                     i 
                                  
                                for all  
                                  
                                     a 
                                     ∈ 
                                     A 
                                  
                               . Thus,  
                                  
                                     inf 
                                     0̸ 
                                     = 
                                     i 
                                  
                               .  □

As we have seen in Section 4.3 in MSDT1, this lattice  
                            
                               ( 
                               A 
                               , 
                               ⪯ 
                               ) 
                            
                          gives the join and meet binary operations  
                            
                               a 
                               ∨ 
                               b 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     sup 
                                  
                                  
                                     ⪯ 
                                  
                               
                               
                               
                                  { 
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  } 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               a 
                               ∧ 
                               b 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     inf 
                                  
                                  
                                     ⪯ 
                                  
                               
                               
                               
                                  { 
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  } 
                               
                            
                          on  
                            A 
                         , respectively.  
                            
                               ( 
                               A 
                               , 
                               ∨ 
                               , 
                               ∧ 
                               ) 
                            
                          is an algebraic lattice. That is,  
                            ∨ 
                          and  
                            ∧ 
                          on  
                            A 
                          are commutative and associative, and they satisfy the absorption laws.

We show that this lattice is distributive.

Theorem 4 
                            
                               The algebraic lattice  
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     A 
                                     , 
                                     ∨ 
                                     , 
                                     ∧ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               with corresponding order-theoretic lattice  
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     A 
                                     , 
                                     ⪯ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               is a distributive lattice, that is,  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           a 
                                           ∧ 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              b 
                                              ∨ 
                                              c 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           = 
                                           
                                              (

                                              b 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           ∨ 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              a

                                              c 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           
                                           
                                           for all  
                                           a 
                                           , 
                                           b 
                                           , 
                                           c 
                                           ∈ 
                                           A 
                                           .

The distributivity identity for the arbitrary points  
                                  a 
                               ,  
                                  b 
                               , and  
                                  c 
                                is equivalent to the conjunction of the two  
                                  min 
                               – 
                                  max 
                                equalities in  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           λ 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                        
                                           a 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           λ 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                        
                                           b 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           λ 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                        
                                           c 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           λ 
                                        
                                        
                                           3 
                                        
                                        
                                           a 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           λ 
                                        
                                        
                                           3 
                                        
                                        
                                           b 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           λ 
                                        
                                        
                                           3 
                                        
                                        
                                           c 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               , respectively,  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       min 
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          { 
                                                          
                                                             
                                                                λ 
                                                             
                                                             
                                                                1 
                                                             
                                                             
                                                                a 
                                                             
                                                          
                                                          , 
                                                          max 
                                                          
                                                          
                                                             { 
                                                             
                                                                
                                                                   λ 
                                                                
                                                                
                                                                   1 
                                                                
                                                                
                                                                   b 
                                                                
                                                             
                                                             , 
                                                             
                                                                
                                                                   λ 
                                                                
                                                                
                                                                   1 
                                                                
                                                                
                                                                   c

A few results are recapitulated. With the Euclidean norm induced by the canonical scalar product, the measures  
                            
                               S 
                               D 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               S 
                               
                                  
                                     D 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                            
                          in  Definitions 12 and 14  coincide, yielding the coefficient of self-determination described in Section 2.3 in MSDT1. The affine space partial orders of self-determination in  Definition 19  are the strong, weak, and general self-determination relations defined in Section 3.3 in MSDT1. The polar coordinates are reproduced. Since  
                            
                               i 
                               ⋅ 
                               e 
                               = 
                               0 
                            
                         , the vectors  
                            
                               
                                  i 
                                  p 
                               
                               ¯

                                  e 
                                  p 
                               
                               ¯ 
                            
                          are orthogonal. The radial and angular coordinates defined in affine space in  Definition 24  are those of the real representation introduced in Section 3.4 in MSDT1. With  Definitions 25 and 26  and  Theorem 2  in affine space representation, we reobtain the radial and angular line segments and their connection to self-determination of the real representation presented in Sections 3.4 and 4.1 in MSDT1.

There is ambiguity in the choice of primitives (Section  3.1 ). However, all possible choices of primitive points imply equivalent representations, in the sense that the resulting simplexes are, naturally, isomorphic.

                            The statements are obvious. Injectivity of

The statements are obvious. Injectivity of

Corollary 2 
                            
                               Any motivation space of affine space representation obtained for any choice of primitive points is canonically isomorphic to the  
                               
                                  2 
                               
                               -simplex  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              R 
                                           
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                
                               ( Section  5.2 
                               ) of the real representation. The latter is a simplest choice.  □

Self-determination theory (SDT) is an important theory of motivation. Albeit widely accepted as a substantial theory, the mathematical approach to SDT is novel. A thorough mathematical analysis of SDT’s concepts, assumptions, and their properties has not been addressed so far. With “Mathematical self-determination theory I: Real representation” ( Ünlü, 2023  MSDT1) and “Mathematical self-determination theory II: Affine space representation” (this paper, MSDT2), we hope to have contributed to the mathematical foundations of this very practical theory.

In Section  1 , we have motivated this paper, and in Section  2 , we have reviewed the basic concepts of affine geometry needed for our work. We briefly summarize the rest of this second paper of the series, and conclude with a resume.

In Section  3 , we have reformulated SDT in more abstract affine spaces. The primitive assumption or starting point of the generalization are three affinely independent points in an affine space (Section  3.1 ), representing the basic postulate of locus of causality in SDT. In particular, this postulate is not treated as a single continuous latent variable “ 
                            
                               L 
                               ∈ 
                               R 
                            
                         ”, which is commonly the case, but is rather differentiated into three latent variables  
                            
                               
                                  L 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                         ,  
                            
                               
                                  L 
                               
                               
                                  e 
                               
                            
                         , and  
                            
                               
                                  L 
                               
                               
                                  p 
                               
                            
                         , the internal, external, and impersonal loci types, standardized “ 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     L 
                                  
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  [ 
                                  0 
                                  , 
                                  1 
                                  ] 
                               
                            
                         ” and complementary  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     L 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               + 
                               
                                  
                                     L 
                                  
                                  
                                     e 
                                  
                               
                               + 
                               
                                  
                                     L 
                                  
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               1 
                            
                         . Everything else of the representation is defined in or derived from these primitives. For example, given the primitives, we define the motivation space of the representation geometrically to be the  
                            2 
                         -simplex spanned by the primitive points in the affine space ( Definition 6 ).

We have discussed the regulated and graded motivation cases (Sections  3.2 and 3.3 ), without and with amotivation, respectively, in affine spaces. Two measures of self-determination were proposed for regulations of a regulated motivation space, one expressible in the barycentric Euclidean distance ( Definition 12 ), the other in the induced metric of a normed affine space ( Definition 14 ). In this paper, the affine space formulation of graded motivation is the most general (mathematical) model of SDT that we studied. It includes the ternary model, the complete formulation in reals, presented in Section 3 in MSDT1.

Self-determination relations on regulated and graded motivation spaces were obtained, with total orders for the former and partial orders for the latter (Section  3.4 ). Note that on the real regulated motivation space, the unit  
                            1 
                         -simplex (Section 2.2 in MSDT1), the self-determination total orders defined by  
                            
                               S 
                               D 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               S 
                               
                                  
                                     D 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
 ( Definition 18 ) and the strong and general self-determination partial orders ( Definition 19 ) coincide, thereby yielding the same total order. In this case, the weak self-determination relation is the diagonal of reflexive regulation pairs (of the unit  
                            1 
                         -simplex), and thus, it is different from the latter total order.

In Section  4 , we have introduced polar coordinates for the study of motivation, in Euclidean affine spaces (Section  4.1 ) that come with a scalar product  
                            
                               〈 
                               , 
                               〉 
                            
                         . Polar coordinates (Section  4.2 ) were derived under the additional assumption of orthogonality of translation vectors determined by the primitives  
                            
                               
                                  〈 
                                  
                                     
                                        i 
                                        p 
                                     
                                     ¯ 
                                  
                                  ,

                                        p 
                                     
                                     ¯ 
                                  
                                  〉 
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                            
                         . Establishing a polar coordinate system is a rather technical issue (in particular,  Theorem 1 ). However, having defined radial and angular line segments in affine space polar coordinates ( Definitions 25 and 26 ), we have shown (Section  4.3 ) that the strong, weak, and general self-determination relations for a graded motivation space of a Euclidean affine space can be total orders on these line segments ( Theorem 2 ).

Section  5  serves to briefly outline interrelations of the real and affine space representations. Obviously, results obtained in one representation translate to the other, from affine to real trivially, such as the distributivity of the general self-determination lattice (Section  5.1 ). Even though in reals a special case has been studied, the real Euclidean affine space of dimension  
                            
                               n 
                               = 
                               2 
                            
                         
 (Section  5.2 ), we think that anchoring and structuring this series of papers around it can only be useful, from an application viewpoint. The real representation is the simpler, direct, and complete conceptualization of the theory, better suited to non-mathematically oriented readers. Mathematically, the representation in an affine space is free in the choice of the primitive points. However, all possible choices for the primitives lead to equivalent representations. The resulting motivation spaces are, in a canonical way, structurally identical (Section  5.3 ).

In further work, statistical applications are possible. Examples are clustering, classification, or parametric statistical inference.

A partition of the motivation space (cf. Section  3.3 ) could be used for cluster and classification analysis. The regulation types partitioning the motivation space could determine the latent typology assumed to underlie the population of reference. For instance, under a latent class model, every person of the population, always assumed to possess her/his regulation, would be in exactly one of the latent regulation types. Forming a partition, the regulation types are mutually exclusive and exhaustive, accounting for the set of all feasible regulations. We would have to further assume that each regulation type has a probability of occurrence greater than zero. This does not follow from the partition of the space. However, for that probability to be positive, necessarily, the regulation type must be non-vacuous, as is the case with a partition. Then, latent class analyses could estimate the prevalences of the regulation types in the population, their conditional probabilities in indicator variables of the data set, and classify respondents of a sample into the motivation regulation types of the partition.

In parametric statistical inference (cf. Section  3.4 ), the

The discrepancies between these sample and population partial orders can be evaluated, using evaluation criteria such as the symmetric difference distance between these relations. In particular, in contrast to order constrained statistical inference, those estimators of the parametric approach necessarily satisfy a partial order structure, such that properties like transitivity or antisymmetry cannot be directly violated in data.

In resume, by contributing the two papers, MSDT1 and MSDT2, we hope to have established a basis not only for theoretical work on the foundations of SDT but also for statistical work. Future motivation research may utilize the mathematical definitions and results, in particular, for theory development, questionnaires, and data analysis.

The author thanks the Associate Editor and three reviewers for their critical and valuable comments and suggestions.
